So, I'll need to implement the Node class, the DoublyLinkedList class, and manage the hashmaps and TreeSet.

Wait, but in Java, it's better to have the Node as an inner class. So, perhaps:

class LFUCache {
    private class Node {
        int key;
        int value;
        int frequency;
        Node prev;
        Node next;
        Node(int k, int v, int f) {
            key = k;
            value = v;
            frequency = f;
        }
    }

    private class DoublyLinkedList {
        Node head;
        Node tail;
        void addFirst(Node node) {
            if (head == null) {
                head = node;
                tail = node;
            } else {
                node.next = head;
                head.prev = node;
                head = node;
            }
        }
        void remove(Node node) {
            if (node.prev != null) {
                node.prev.next = node.next;
            } else {
                head = node.next;
            }
            if (node.next != null) {
                node.next.prev = node.prev;
            } else {
                tail = node.prev;
            }
        }
        Node removeLast() {
            Node last = tail;
            if (last != null) {
                if (last.prev != null) {
                    last.prev.next = null;
                    tail = last.prev;
                } else {
                    head = null;
                    tail = null;
                }
            }
            return last;
        }
        boolean isEmpty() {
            return head == null;
        }
    }

    private HashMap<Integer, Node> keyToNode;
    private HashMap<Integer, DoublyLinkedList> freqToNodes;
    private TreeSet<Integer> frequencies;
    private int capacity;
    private int minFreq;

    public LFUCache(int capacity) {
        this.capacity = capacity;
        keyToNode = new HashMap<>();
        freqToNodes = new HashMap<>();
        frequencies = new TreeSet<>();
        if (capacity == 0) {
            // handle case where capacity is 0, but according to constraints, capacity >=1?
            // Wait, problem statement says 1 <= capacity <= 1e4, so no need to handle 0.
        }
    }

    public int get(int key) {
        if (!keyToNode.containsKey(key)) {
            return -1;
        }
        Node node = keyToNode.get(key);
        int value = node.value;
        // increment frequency
        node.frequency++;
        // remove from current frequency list
        int oldFreq = node.frequency - 1;
        DoublyLinkedList oldList = freqToNodes.get(oldFreq);
        oldList.remove(node);
        if (oldList.isEmpty()) {
            freqToNodes.remove(oldFreq);
            frequencies.remove(oldFreq);
        }
        // add to new frequency list
        int newFreq = node.frequency;
        DoublyLinkedList newList = freqToNodes.get(newFreq);
        if (newList == null) {
            newList = new DoublyLinkedList();
            freqToNodes.put(newFreq, newList);
            frequencies.add(newFreq);
        }
        newList.addFirst(node);
        return value;
    }

    public void put(int key, int value) {
        if (capacity == 0) return; // assuming capacity is at least 1 as per problem statement.

        if (keyToNode.containsKey(key)) {
            Node node = keyToNode.get(key);
            node.value = value;
            node.frequency++;
            int oldFreq = node.frequency - 1;
            DoublyLinkedList oldList = freqToNodes.get(oldFreq);
            oldList.remove(node);
            if (oldList.isEmpty()) {
                freqToNodes.remove(oldFreq);
                frequencies.remove(oldFreq);
            }
            int newFreq = node.frequency;
            DoublyLinkedList newList = freqToNodes.get(newFreq);
            if (newList == null) {
                newList = new DoublyLinkedList();
                freqToNodes.put(newFreq, newList);
                frequencies.add(newFreq);
            }
            newList.addFirst(node);
        } else {
            if (keyToNode.size() >= capacity) {
                // Evict the LFU key
                int minFreq = frequencies.first();
                DoublyLinkedList minList = freqToNodes.get(minFreq);
                Node lruNode = minList.removeLast();
                keyToNode.remove(lruNode.key);
                if (minList.isEmpty()) {
                    freqToNodes.remove(minFreq);
                    frequencies.remove(minFreq);
                }
            }
            // Add new node
            Node newNode = new Node(key, value, 1);
            keyToNode.put(key, newNode);
            DoublyLinkedList newList = freqToNodes.get(1);
            if (newList == null) {
                newList = new DoublyLinkedList();
                freqToNodes.put(1, newList);
                frequencies.add(1);
            }
            newList.addFirst(newNode);
        }
    }
}

Each get and put operation involves:

- For get:
   - O(1) to check if key exists.

- For put when key doesn't exist:
   - O(1) to check if cache is full.
   - If full, O(1) to get minFreq, O(1) to remove the last node from minFreq list.
   - O(1) to add new node.

So, each operation is O(1) average time.

The space complexity is O(capacity), since we store each key once.

Energy implications: Since each operation is O(1), and the number of operations is up to 2e5, the energy usage is LOW.

To solve this problem, we need to design and implement a Least Frequently Used (LFU) cache. The LFU cache should efficiently handle insertion, retrieval, and eviction of keys based on their usage frequency. When the cache reaches its capacity, it should evict the least frequently used key. In case of a tie (multiple keys with the same frequency), the least recently used key should be evicted.

### Approach
The approach involves using a combination of hash maps and doubly linked lists to efficiently manage the keys and their frequencies. Here's a detailed breakdown of the approach:

1. **Data Structures**:
   - **HashMap (keyToNode)**: This map stores each key and its associated node, which contains the value, frequency, and pointers to the previous and next nodes in the linked list.
   - **HashMap (freqToNodes)**: This map stores the frequency as the key and a doubly linked list of nodes as the value. Each linked list maintains nodes in the order of their last access, with the most recently accessed node at the front.
   - **TreeSet (frequencies)**: This set keeps track of all unique frequencies present in the cache, allowing efficient retrieval of the minimum frequency.

2. **Operations**:
   - **get(int key)**: Retrieves the value of the key if it exists, increments its frequency, and updates its position in the linked list to reflect recent access.
   - **put(int key, int value)**: Inserts or updates the key-value pair. If the key exists, its value and frequency are updated. If the key does not exist and the cache is full, the least frequently used key is evicted, and the new key is added.

3. **Eviction**:
   - When the cache is full, the key with the smallest frequency is evicted. If multiple keys have the same smallest frequency, the least recently used key (at the end of the linked list) is evicted.

### Solution Code
```java
import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.TreeSet;

class LFUCache {
    private class Node {
        int key;
        int value;
        int frequency;
        Node prev;
        Node next;
        Node(int k, int v, int f) {
            key = k;
            value = v;
            frequency = f;
        }
    }

    private class DoublyLinkedList {
        Node head;
        Node tail;
        void addFirst(Node node) {
            if (head == null) {
                head = node;
                tail = node;
            } else {
                node.next = head;
                head.prev = node;
                head = node;
            }
        }
        void remove(Node node) {
            if (node.prev != null) {
                node.prev.next = node.next;
            } else {
                head = node.next;
            }
            if (node.next != null) {
                node.next.prev = node.prev;
            } else {
                tail = node.prev;
            }
        }
        Node removeLast() {
            Node last = tail;
            if (last != null) {
                if (last.prev != null) {
                    last.prev.next = null;
                    tail = last.prev;
                } else {
                    head = null;
                    tail = null;
                }
            }
            return last;
        }
        boolean isEmpty() {
            return head == null;
        }
    }

    private HashMap<Integer, Node> keyToNode;
    private HashMap<Integer, DoublyLinkedList> freqToNodes;
    private TreeSet<Integer> frequencies;
    private int capacity;

    public LFUCache(int capacity) {
        this.capacity = capacity;
        keyToNode = new HashMap<>();
        freqToNodes = new HashMap<>();
        frequencies = new TreeSet<>();
    }

    public int get(int key) {
        if (!keyToNode.containsKey(key)) {
            return -1;
        }
        Node node = keyToNode.get(key);
        int value = node.value;
        int oldFreq = node.frequency;
        node.frequency++;
        int newFreq = node.frequency;

        DoublyLinkedList oldList = freqToNodes.get(oldFreq);
        oldList.remove(node);
        if (oldList.isEmpty()) {
            freqToNodes.remove(oldFreq);
            frequencies.remove(oldFreq);
        }

        DoublyLinkedList newList = freqToNodes.get(newFreq);
        if (newList == null) {
            newList = new DoublyLinkedList();
            freqToNodes.put(newFreq, newList);
            frequencies.add(newFreq);
        }
        newList.addFirst(node);

        return value;
    }

    public void put(int key, int value) {
        if (capacity == 0) return;

        if (keyToNode.containsKey(key)) {
            Node node = keyToNode.get(key);
            node.value = value;
            int oldFreq = node.frequency;
            node.frequency++;
            int newFreq = node.frequency;

            DoublyLinkedList oldList = freqToNodes.get(oldFreq);
            oldList.remove(node);
            if (oldList.isEmpty()) {
                freqToNodes.remove(oldFreq);
                frequencies.remove(oldFreq);
            }

            DoublyLinkedList newList = freqToNodes.get(newFreq);
            if (newList == null) {
                newList = new DoublyLinkedList();
                freqToNodes.put(newFreq, newList);
                frequencies.add(newFreq);
            }
            newList.addFirst(node);
        } else {
            if (keyToNode.size() >= capacity) {
                int minFreq = frequencies.first();
                DoublyLinkedList minList = freqToNodes.get(minFreq);
                Node lruNode = minList.removeLast();
                keyToNode.remove(lruNode.key);
                if (minList.isEmpty()) {
                    freqToNodes.remove(minFreq);
                    frequencies.remove(minFreq);
                }
            }

            Node newNode = new Node(key, value, 1);
            keyToNode.put(key, newNode);
            DoublyLinkedList newList = freqToNodes.get(1);
            if (newList == null) {
                newList = new DoublyLinkedList();
                freqToNodes.put(1, newList);
                frequencies.add(1);
            }
            newList.addFirst(newNode);
        }
    }
}
```

### Explanation
- **Data Structures**: The solution uses hash maps to store key-value pairs and their frequencies, and doubly linked lists to maintain the order of access within each frequency group. A tree set is used to efficiently track the minimum frequency.
- **Operations**: Both `get` and `put` operations are designed to run in O(1) average time by efficiently updating frequencies and adjusting the position of nodes within linked lists.
- **Eviction**: When the cache is full, the least frequently used key is evicted by retrieving the smallest frequency from the tree set and removing the least recently used key from the corresponding linked list.

This approach ensures that the cache operations are efficient and meet the problem constraints.
